enrichment.short_description: Salesloft is an AI-powered sales engagement and revenue orchestration platform that helps B2B sales teams prospect, manage deals, and forecast revenue from a single workspace.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription with custom, quote-based per-seat pricing; no public price list
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, contact sales - Salesloft does not publish list prices
enrichment.company_size: Approximately 1,000 to 1,200 employees as of 2026, following the December 2025 merger with Clari
enrichment.seo_title: Salesloft Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Salesloft costs in 2026, how its Essentials, Advanced, and Premier plans compare, and whether this AI sales engagement platform fits your team.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salesloft is a sales engagement and revenue orchestration platform founded in September 2011 in Atlanta, Georgia, by Kyle Porter and David Cummings, who met at Georgia Tech's Advanced Technology Development Center; Cummings had previously founded the marketing automation company Pardot. The company grew from an early-stage startup backed by roughly 1 million dollars in seed funding and a Techstars accelerator cohort into one of the most widely used platforms in the sales engagement category, with reported figures around 1,000 to 1,200 employees and thousands of customers as of 2026. In December 2025, Salesloft completed a merger with revenue intelligence company Clari, with Clari's Steve Cox becoming CEO of the combined organization, positioning the merged company as a unified Predictive Revenue System that pairs Salesloft's engagement and conversation intelligence tools with Clari's forecasting stack. The core Salesloft platform centers on sales cadences, multi-step, multi-channel outreach sequences across email, phone, and social touches, a built-in dialer, email tracking and templates, conversation intelligence that records and analyzes sales calls, deal management, and pipeline and forecasting tools historically enhanced through integrations. Salesloft does not publish list pricing on its website; prospective customers must request a custom quote based on seat count, contract term, and selected modules. Third-party buyer intelligence sources report three 2026 plan tiers, commonly named Essentials, Advanced, and Premier, with real per-seat costs cited anywhere from about 75 dollars to over 200 dollars per user per month depending on tier, volume, and add-ons; the dialer and Conversations, its call recording and intelligence add-on, carry additional per-seat fees that can meaningfully increase total contract value. Because pricing is opaque and quote-based, buyers typically need to negotiate and benchmark against peer data to avoid overpaying. Salesloft is best suited for mid-market and enterprise B2B sales organizations that need a structured, repeatable outbound process, unified activity tracking across email and calls, and visibility into deal and rep performance, rather than very small teams seeking a low-cost, self-serve tool. Competitors in the sales engagement space include Outreach, Apollo.io, and Groove, while its expanded scope after the Clari merger also places it in competition with dedicated revenue intelligence and forecasting vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salesloft is a sales engagement and revenue orchestration platform founded in 2011 in Atlanta, Georgia, by Kyle Porter and David Cummings. It gives B2B sales teams a single workspace to run outbound prospecting cadences, track buyer engagement across email and calls, manage deals, and forecast revenue, and it expanded its scope significantly after completing a merger with revenue intelligence company Clari in December 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Salesloft organizes outreach into cadences, multi-step sequences that blend email, phone calls, and social touches so reps follow a consistent, repeatable process instead of ad hoc outreach.

Beyond prospecting, the platform adds conversation intelligence for call recording and coaching, Deal Rooms and Deal Insights for pipeline visibility, and AI-driven next-action prioritization, with native two-way integrations into Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics, and HubSpot.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Salesloft does not publish list pricing on its website; all plans are quoted individually based on seat count, contract length, and selected modules across three commonly cited tiers, Essentials, Advanced, and Premier.

Third-party buyer intelligence sources report per-seat costs ranging from roughly 75 dollars to over 200 dollars per user per month depending on tier and add-ons, with extras such as the dialer or Conversations billed separately and capable of adding meaningfully to total contract value.

features.title: Sales Cadences
features.description: Multi-step, multi-channel outreach sequences combining email, phone calls, and social touches to standardize prospecting.

features.title: Built-in Dialer
features.description: Click-to-call calling directly inside the platform with call recording and logging.

features.title: Conversation Intelligence
features.description: AI-driven call recording, transcription, and analysis that surfaces talk tracks, objections, and coaching moments.

features.title: Deal Management
features.description: Deal Rooms and Deal Insights that centralize buyer engagement and deal risk signals.

features.title: Email Tracking and Templates
features.description: Open and click tracking, dynamic personalization, and a shared template library for reps.

features.title: Forecasting and Analytics
features.description: Pipeline and revenue forecasting dashboards, expanded through the Clari integration after the December 2025 merger.

features.title: AI Prioritization
features.description: AI-suggested next actions that tell reps who to contact and when, based on buyer engagement signals.

features.title: CRM and Tooling Integrations
features.description: Native, two-way sync with Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics, HubSpot, and other CRMs plus a wide app marketplace.

pros: Deep, mature feature set for structured, repeatable outbound sales processes across email, calls, and social
Conversation intelligence and deal insight tools give managers visibility into rep and deal health, not just activity counts
Strong native integrations with Salesforce and other major CRMs
Post-merger roadmap with Clari adds enterprise-grade forecasting on top of engagement data
cons: Pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation, making cost comparison difficult for buyers
Third-party buying data suggests per-seat costs and add-ons like the dialer or Conversations can add up quickly for larger teams
Steeper learning curve and implementation effort than lightweight, self-serve sales tools
Best value is realized by larger sales organizations, so it can be costly for very small teams

faqs.question: What is Salesloft used for?
faqs.answer: Salesloft is a sales engagement platform used by B2B sales teams to run structured outbound prospecting cadences across email and phone, track buyer engagement, record and analyze sales calls, manage deals, and forecast revenue.

faqs.question: How much does Salesloft cost?
faqs.answer: Salesloft does not publish list pricing. It sells custom, quote-based subscriptions typically organized into Essentials, Advanced, and Premier tiers, with per-seat costs that third-party sources report ranging from roughly 75 dollars to over 200 dollars per user per month depending on tier, contract term, and add-ons.

faqs.question: When was Salesloft founded?
faqs.answer: Salesloft was incorporated in September 2011 in Atlanta, Georgia, by Kyle Porter and David Cummings.

faqs.question: Did Salesloft merge with another company?
faqs.answer: Yes. Salesloft completed a merger with revenue intelligence company Clari on December 3, 2025, with Clari's Steve Cox becoming CEO of the combined organization.

faqs.question: What is Salesloft's Conversation Intelligence feature?
faqs.answer: It is an AI-powered call recording and analysis tool that transcribes sales calls, highlights talk tracks and objections, and helps managers coach reps based on real conversation data.

faqs.question: Who are Salesloft's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Salesloft competes primarily with Outreach in the sales engagement category, along with tools like Apollo.io and Groove; its expanded scope after the Clari merger also brings it into competition with dedicated forecasting and revenue intelligence vendors.

faqs.question: Does Salesloft integrate with Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Salesloft offers native, two-way integration with Salesforce, along with integrations for Microsoft Dynamics, HubSpot, and other CRMs.

faqs.question: Is Salesloft suitable for small businesses?
faqs.answer: Salesloft is generally built for mid-market and enterprise B2B sales teams that need a structured outbound process; its quote-based pricing and feature depth make it less cost-effective for very small teams compared with lighter, self-serve sales tools.

enrichment.short_description: Salesmate is an AI-powered, all-in-one CRM for sales, marketing, and customer support teams, featuring native calling, texting, and automation.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered subscription, priced per user per month
enrichment.starting_price: $23 USD per user per month (Basic plan, billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: Salesmate CRM Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Salesmate CRM pricing plans, built-in calling features, and its Sandy AI assistant to see if this all-in-one CRM fits your sales team in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salesmate is an AI-powered customer relationship management platform built by Rapidops Inc., a software company headquartered at 1422 South Tryon Street in Charlotte, North Carolina. Salesmate was founded in 2016 by Samir Motwani, with Dipesh Patel as co-founder, and the product is positioned as an all-in-one CRM covering sales pipeline management, marketing automation, and customer support for small and mid-sized businesses. Unlike many CRMs that charge extra for calling functionality, Salesmate natively bundles built-in calling, texting, power dialing, voicemail drop, and call transcription into its paid plans, alongside contact and deal management, email sync and tracking, a meeting scheduler, workflow automation called smart flows, and custom reporting dashboards. The platform also includes an AI layer built around its Sandy AI assistant, plus newer AI agents aimed at automating lead qualification, meeting booking, and workflow triggers. Salesmate is priced on a tiered, per-user, per-month basis with four public tiers: Basic at 23 dollars per user per month, covering contact and deal management, calendar sync, email tracking, and 5,000 monthly smart flow credits; Pro at 39 dollars per user per month, adding sequences, team inbox, ticketing, custom dashboards, single sign-on, and 10,000 smart flow credits; Business at 63 dollars per user per month billed annually or 79 dollars billed monthly, adding power dialing, voicemail drop, custom modules, and 15,000 smart flow credits; and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier with audit logs, dedicated onboarding and account management, IP restrictions, and 24/7 support. Annual billing saves up to roughly 20 percent versus monthly billing, and Salesmate offers a 15-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required. Add-ons such as calling and texting minutes, extra automation credits, dedicated IP addresses, and AI agent credits are billed separately from the base subscription. Salesmate targets small and mid-market sales, marketing, and support teams that want a single, moderately priced platform with native communication tools rather than needing to bolt on a separate dialer or help desk product, competing most directly with CRMs such as Pipedrive, HubSpot, and Zoho CRM.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salesmate is an AI-powered CRM built by Rapidops Inc., founded in 2016 and headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina. It combines sales pipeline management, marketing automation, and light customer support tools in one platform aimed at small and mid-sized businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Salesmate natively bundles calling, texting, power dialing, and voicemail drop into its higher-tier plans, avoiding the need for a separate telephony add-on that many competing CRMs require.

The platform also includes deal pipeline management, email sequences, a meeting scheduler, workflow automation credits called smart flows, and an AI layer built around the Sandy AI assistant and newer AI agents for lead qualification and meeting booking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Salesmate offers four tiers: Basic at 23 dollars per user per month, Pro at 39 dollars, Business at 63 dollars per user per month billed annually or 79 dollars billed monthly, and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier.
Annual billing saves up to roughly 20 percent compared with monthly billing, and a 15-day free trial with full feature access is available with no credit card required.

pros: Native calling, texting, and power dialer functionality included without a separate paid add-on at higher tiers
Competitive per-user pricing starting at 23 dollars per month compared with many enterprise CRMs
Built-in AI assistant and automation tools aimed at reducing manual data entry
15-day free trial with full feature access and no credit card required
cons: Entry-level Basic plan lacks sequences, team inbox, and AI features, which are gated to Pro and above
Power dialer and voicemail drop are limited to the Business tier and above
Smart flow automation is credit-limited per plan, which can constrain heavy automation users
Smaller third-party integration ecosystem than larger CRMs like Salesforce or HubSpot

faqs.question: What is Salesmate used for?
faqs.answer: Salesmate is an AI-powered CRM used by small and mid-sized businesses to manage sales pipelines, automate outreach, place calls and texts, and handle basic customer support from one platform.

faqs.question: How much does Salesmate cost?
faqs.answer: Salesmate starts at 23 dollars per user per month for the Basic plan, with Pro at 39 dollars, Business at 63 dollars per user per month billed annually (79 dollars billed monthly), and a custom-quoted Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: Who owns Salesmate?
faqs.answer: Salesmate is a product of Rapidops Inc., founded in 2016 by Samir Motwani and Dipesh Patel, headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina.

faqs.question: Does Salesmate include a built-in dialer?
faqs.answer: Yes, calling, texting, power dialing, and voicemail drop are built into the Business and Enterprise plans, with basic calling and email available on lower tiers.

faqs.question: Does Salesmate offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Salesmate offers a 15-day free trial with full access to features and no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is Sandy AI?
faqs.answer: Sandy AI is Salesmate's built-in AI assistant, available from the Pro plan up, complemented by newer AI agents for tasks like lead qualification and meeting booking.

faqs.question: Does Salesmate support annual billing discounts?
faqs.answer: Yes, annual billing saves up to approximately 20 percent compared with monthly billing.

faqs.question: What does the Enterprise plan include?
faqs.answer: The Enterprise plan adds audit logs, dedicated onboarding and account management, IP restriction, quarterly business reviews, and 24/7 support on top of custom pricing.

enrichment.short_description: SalesScreen is a sales gamification and performance management platform that turns CRM activity into real-time leaderboards, competitions, and rewards.
enrichment.pricing_model: Tiered, team-size-gated subscription priced per month
enrichment.starting_price: From $250 USD per month for the Scale plan, minimum 10 users
enrichment.seo_title: SalesScreen Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how SalesScreen's Scale and Pro plans price out, what sales gamification features are included, and whether it fits your sales team's motivation strategy.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SalesScreen is a sales gamification and performance management platform headquartered in Oslo, Norway, with additional offices in Barcelona, Spain and New York City; more than a third of its revenue reportedly comes from the North American market. The company traces its roots to 2011, when it began as a consultancy called Dogu before pivoting into the dedicated sales gamification product now known as SalesScreen. The platform is designed to help sales managers keep reps motivated and focused by turning CRM activity data into real-time dashboards, leaderboards, and competitions; it connects natively to CRMs such as Salesforce and HubSpot so that calls, deals, and other sales activities sync automatically into the gamification layer. Core capabilities include customizable performance metrics and targets, an interactive social feed for celebrating wins, dozens of pre-built sales competitions and games, milestone and achievement recognition, a redeemable reward shop with physical prizes, and coaching or scorecard tools sold as an add-on for roughly 5 dollars per user per month on any plan. SalesScreen uses a tiered, team-size-gated pricing structure rather than a simple per-seat model: an Essentials tier is available as a 30-day free trial requiring no credit card, a Scale plan starts at 250 dollars per month for a minimum of 10 users, about 25 dollars per user per month, and is the most commonly recommended tier, a Pro plan starts at 675 dollars per month for a minimum of 15 users, about 45 dollars per user per month, and unlocks features like metric funnels, battles, and premium integrations, and an Enterprise plan offers custom pricing with a private database and advanced access management for larger organizations. SalesScreen is best suited to sales organizations of 10 or more reps, such as inside sales, field sales, and call center teams, that already use Salesforce or HubSpot and want to layer motivation, transparency, and recognition on top of their existing pipeline data rather than replace their CRM outright.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SalesScreen is a sales gamification and performance management platform headquartered in Oslo, Norway, with roots dating back to 2011 as a consultancy called Dogu, plus offices in Barcelona and New York. It helps sales managers keep reps motivated by turning CRM activity into real-time dashboards, competitions, and rewards.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SalesScreen syncs natively with Salesforce and HubSpot so that calls, deals, and other activities feed real-time leaderboards, achievements, and an interactive social feed without manual data entry.
Higher tiers add configurable metric targets, metric funnels, head-to-head battles, and a redeemable reward shop, with coaching and scorecards available as a separate add-on.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SalesScreen's Essentials tier is a free 30-day trial, the Scale plan starts at 250 dollars per month for a minimum of 10 users, and the Pro plan starts at 675 dollars per month for a minimum of 15 users, with a custom-quoted Enterprise tier above that.
An optional Coaching and Scorecards add-on is available on any plan for roughly 5 dollars per user per month.

features.title: Real-Time Leaderboards and Dashboards
features.description: Live displays of rep and team performance pulled automatically from connected CRMs.

features.description: Pre-built and customizable competitions and games that run over set time windows to drive short-term motivation.

features.description: Automated recognition for hitting personal or team milestones, shown in an interactive social feed.

features.title: Reward Shop
features.description: A redeemable rewards catalog where reps can exchange earned points for real prizes.

features.title: Metric Targets and Funnels
features.description: Configurable targets on tracked metrics and funnel views for sales activities, available from Scale and Pro tiers.

features.title: Battles
features.description: Head-to-head or team-versus-team competitions available on the Pro plan and above.

features.title: Coaching and Scorecards Add-on
features.description: An optional add-on, priced separately per user, for structured coaching and performance scorecards.

features.title: Native CRM Integrations
features.description: Real-time sync with Salesforce and HubSpot, plus additional premium integrations.

pros: Deep, purpose-built gamification feature set beyond simple leaderboards, including competitions, achievements, and a physical reward shop
Native, real-time integrations with Salesforce and HubSpot keep dashboards current without manual data entry
Free 30-day Essentials trial with no credit card required
Global presence with offices in Oslo, Barcelona, and New York supporting customers across regions
cons: Paid plans are gated by minimum team size, 10 users for Scale and 15 for Pro, making it less economical for very small teams
Advanced features like metric funnels, battles, and premium integrations are locked to higher tiers
Coaching and scorecards require a separate per-user add-on rather than being included
Enterprise-level features like a private database require custom Enterprise pricing

faqs.question: What is SalesScreen used for?
faqs.answer: SalesScreen is a sales gamification and performance management platform that turns CRM activity data into real-time leaderboards, competitions, and rewards to keep sales teams motivated and focused.

faqs.question: How much does SalesScreen cost?
faqs.answer: SalesScreen's Scale plan starts at 250 dollars per month for a minimum of 10 users, and the Pro plan starts at 675 dollars per month for a minimum of 15 users; an Essentials tier is available as a free 30-day trial, and Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Where is SalesScreen based?
faqs.answer: SalesScreen is headquartered in Oslo, Norway, with additional offices in Barcelona, Spain and New York City.

faqs.question: When was SalesScreen founded?
faqs.answer: SalesScreen traces its origins to 2011, when it began as a consultancy called Dogu before pivoting into the dedicated sales gamification product now known as SalesScreen.

faqs.question: Does SalesScreen integrate with Salesforce and HubSpot?
faqs.answer: Yes, SalesScreen offers native integrations with both Salesforce and HubSpot that sync sales data and activity in real time.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for SalesScreen?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Essentials tier is available as a 30-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is the SalesScreen Coaching and Scorecards add-on?
faqs.answer: It is an optional add-on available on any plan for roughly 5 dollars per user per month that adds structured coaching tools and performance scorecards.

faqs.question: What is the minimum team size for SalesScreen's paid plans?
faqs.answer: The Scale plan requires at least 10 users and the Pro plan requires at least 15 users.

use_cases.audience: Inside sales and remote sales teams

enrichment.short_description: Salut à Toi, now known as Libervia, is a free, decentralized, multi-frontend communication platform built on the XMPP standard, supporting messaging, blogging, calls, and file sharing.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, no cost
enrichment.seo_title: Salut à Toi (Libervia) Review: Features and How It Works
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Salut à Toi, also known as Libervia, a free decentralized XMPP communication platform with multi-frontend messaging, blogging, and encrypted calls.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Salut à Toi, now officially rebranded as Libervia, is a free and libre, decentralized communication platform built entirely on the XMPP, or Jabber, open messaging standard. Development began around 2008 and has been led primarily by French developer Jerome Poisson, known online as Goffi, with contributions from Adrien Cossa and, more recently, funding support from NLnet and the NGI, or Next Generation Internet, initiative, which has accelerated work on features like serverless architectures and metadata reduction for privacy. The project's defining architectural idea is a single shared backend serving many different frontends, so the same account and data can be accessed through a desktop application, a command-line interface, a text user interface, a web client, and mobile-style clients, all built on a Python and Twisted async foundation with SQLAlchemy and Alembic handling data persistence. Because it is XMPP-based, Libervia is inherently federated and decentralized: users are not locked into a single server or vendor and can switch clients while keeping the same account, similar in spirit to email. Beyond one-to-one and group instant messaging, the platform supports end-to-end encryption via OMEMO and OpenPGP, audio and video calls including group conferencing, blogging and microblogging built on the XMPP PubSub extension, encrypted file sharing, decentralized issue tracking and list management, an ActivityPub gateway that lets Libervia accounts interact with Fediverse services such as Mastodon, and gateways that bridge XMPP with IRC, other legacy networks, and email. The project is released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 or later, a copyleft license chosen specifically to keep the surrounding ecosystem free and libre even when the software is run as a network service. As a community and association-driven open-source project rather than a commercial company, Libervia has no pricing, sales team, or corporate headquarters; it is free to self-host or run via existing XMPP infrastructure. It is best suited to privacy-conscious individuals, activists, and organizations that want a single federated identity for messaging, blogging, and calls without depending on a centralized commercial platform, and it is typically compared with other XMPP clients and Fediverse-adjacent decentralized communication tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Salut à Toi, now officially known as Libervia, is a free, decentralized communication platform built on the XMPP standard, developed since around 2008 primarily by French developer Jerome Poisson, known as Goffi, with recent funding from NLnet and the NGI initiative.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Its defining feature is a shared backend that powers many frontends, desktop, web, command-line, and text user interface, from the same account, going well beyond instant messaging to include blogging, encrypted file sharing, and audio/video calls.
Gateways let Libervia bridge to IRC, email, and, through an ActivityPub gateway, Fediverse platforms such as Mastodon, while OMEMO and OpenPGP provide end-to-end encryption for private conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Salut à Toi/Libervia is entirely free and open source, released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 or later, with no subscription plans, company, or sales process; it can be self-hosted or used with any compatible XMPP server.

features.title: Multi-Frontend Architecture
features.description: One shared backend powers desktop, web, command-line, and text user interface frontends from the same account.

features.title: End-to-End Encryption
features.description: Support for OMEMO and OpenPGP encryption for private conversations.

features.title: Audio and Video Calls
features.description: Direct and group audio and video calling built on XMPP extensions.

features.title: Blogging and Microblogging
features.description: PubSub-based blogging and microblogging features built into the same platform as messaging.

features.title: File Sharing
features.description: Encrypted file transfer between contacts.

features.title: ActivityPub Gateway
features.description: A bridge that lets Libervia accounts interact with Fediverse services such as Mastodon.

features.title: Legacy Network Gateways
features.description: Bridges to IRC and email so XMPP users can reach contacts on other networks.

features.title: Decentralized Issue Tracking
features.description: Built-in tools for managing tasks and issue trackers over XMPP PubSub.

pros: Fully free, open-source, and federated, with no vendor lock-in or subscription cost
Unusually broad feature set for an XMPP client, spanning messaging, blogging, calls, and file sharing in one platform
Multi-frontend design lets the same account be used from desktop, web, command-line, or mobile-style interfaces
Backed by NLnet and NGI funding, supporting continued development of privacy features
cons: Long, largely solo-led development history means a smaller contributor base than major messaging platforms
Smaller user base than mainstream chat apps, so network effects and contact discovery are limited
Documentation and interface are primarily oriented toward technically comfortable users
Now branded as Libervia, which can cause confusion for people searching under the older Salut à Toi name

faqs.question: What is Salut à Toi?
faqs.answer: Salut à Toi, now officially known as Libervia, is a free, decentralized, multi-frontend communication platform built on the XMPP standard, supporting instant messaging, blogging, file sharing, and audio/video calls.

faqs.question: Is Salut à Toi free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open-source software released under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 or later.

faqs.question: Who develops Salut à Toi?
faqs.answer: It has been developed primarily by French developer Jerome Poisson, known as Goffi, since around 2008, with contributions from Adrien Cossa and funding support from NLnet and the NGI initiative in recent years.

faqs.question: What does Salut à Toi have to do with Libervia?
faqs.answer: Salut à Toi was rebranded as Libervia; both names refer to the same underlying project and codebase.

faqs.question: What frontends does Salut à Toi support?
faqs.answer: It supports desktop, web, command-line, and text user interface frontends, all sharing the same backend and account.

faqs.question: Does Salut à Toi support encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports end-to-end encryption via both OMEMO and OpenPGP.

faqs.question: Can Salut à Toi communicate with other networks?
faqs.answer: Yes, through gateways it can bridge to IRC, email, and, via an ActivityPub gateway, Fediverse platforms such as Mastodon.

faqs.question: Does Salut à Toi require a company account or subscription?
faqs.answer: No, it is a community and association-run free software project with no company, pricing plans, or subscription requirement.

use_cases.title: Federated Personal Messaging
use_cases.description: Privacy-conscious individuals use Salut à Toi/Libervia as their primary XMPP client for encrypted one-to-one and group messaging without depending on a centralized provider.

use_cases.title: Combined Blogging and Chat
use_cases.description: Users who want to publish blog or microblog posts and chat from the same federated identity use its integrated PubSub-based blogging features.

use_cases.title: Cross-Network Bridging
use_cases.description: Technical users bridge XMPP conversations to IRC, email, or ActivityPub-based networks to consolidate communication in one client.

features: Full access to all frontends: desktop, web, command-line, and text user interface
End-to-end encryption via OMEMO and OpenPGP
Blogging, file sharing, and audio/video calls
No account fees, self-hostable via any XMPP server

enrichment.short_description: SAMA is a free, open-source, self-hostable chat server for mobile and web apps, built on uWebSockets.js with end-to-end encryption and clustering.
enrichment.seo_title: SAMA Chat Server Review: Features and Self-Hosting Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what the open-source SAMA chat server offers, from clustering and encryption to its React and Flutter clients, and how to self-host it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SAMA, whose name is short for Simple but Advanced Messaging Alternative, is a free, open-source chat server designed to give mobile and web developers a self-hostable, real-time messaging backend rather than depending on a proprietary chat-as-a-service vendor. The project is developed by the SAMA-Communications organization on GitHub, and its core server, published as sama-server, is built in JavaScript on top of uWebSockets.js for high-throughput, low-latency WebSocket connections. Around the server, the SAMA-Communications organization maintains a broader stack: a React-based web client called sama-client, a Flutter-based mobile client, and an administrative dashboard for managing the deployment, with the dashboard component published under an MIT license. Key technical features include end-to-end encryption, native clustering for horizontal scalability and high availability, multi-device session support, push notifications, rich media messaging, and user management with authentication, profile editing, and search built in, along with support for both private one-to-one chats and group conversations. Developers who want to try the platform without deploying their own infrastructure can test the full stack against a hosted public instance at app.samacloud.io, and documentation for the REST and WebSocket APIs is published at docs.samacloud.io. Because SAMA is open source and self-hosted by design, it does not have a public pricing page, subscription tiers, or a disclosed founding year, headquarters, or employee count in the way a commercial SaaS vendor would; the project positions itself simply as free, secure, and easy to use. SAMA is best suited to development teams that want to embed real-time chat into an existing mobile or web application while retaining full control over their data and infrastructure, rather than teams that prefer an out-of-the-box, vendor-hosted messaging API. It competes conceptually with other self-hostable or open-source chat infrastructure projects, positioning itself as a lighter-weight, more modern alternative for teams that do not want to build a WebSocket-based messaging layer from scratch.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SAMA, short for Simple but Advanced Messaging Alternative, is a free, open-source chat server maintained by the SAMA-Communications organization on GitHub, built on uWebSockets.js for high-throughput, low-latency real-time messaging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The stack includes the core sama-server, a React-based web client, a Flutter-based mobile client, and an MIT-licensed admin dashboard, with end-to-end encryption, clustering for high availability, multi-device sessions, and push notifications built in.
Developers can evaluate the full stack against a public hosted demo at app.samacloud.io before deciding to self-host, and REST and WebSocket API documentation is published at docs.samacloud.io.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SAMA is free and open source with no published pricing tiers; it is designed to be self-hosted, and a public cloud demo instance is available for testing at no cost.

features.title: WebSocket-Based Chat Server
features.description: Core sama-server built on uWebSockets.js for high-throughput, low-latency real-time messaging.

features.title: End-to-End Encryption

features.title: Clustering and High Availability

features.title: Multi-Device Support
features.description: Sessions and message sync across multiple devices per user.

features.title: Push Notifications
features.description: Built-in push notification support for mobile and web clients.

features.title: Prebuilt Clients
features.description: Official React web client and Flutter mobile client available alongside the server.

features.title: Admin Dashboard
features.description: A dedicated, MIT-licensed administrative dashboard for managing deployments.

features.title: REST and WebSocket APIs
features.description: Documented APIs for building custom chat experiences on top of the server.

pros: Fully open source and self-hostable, giving developers complete control over data and infrastructure
Comes with a full stack out of the box: server, web client, mobile client, and admin dashboard
Built on modern, high-performance WebSocket tooling with uWebSockets.js
Public hosted demo at app.samacloud.io makes it easy to evaluate before self-hosting
cons: No public company information, founding date, or headquarters, making vendor due diligence harder
Smaller community and GitHub following compared with larger open-source chat projects
Self-hosting requires developer resources to deploy, secure, and maintain the server and clustering setup
Documentation is comparatively lightweight versus established commercial chat APIs

faqs.question: What does SAMA stand for?
faqs.answer: SAMA stands for Simple but Advanced Messaging Alternative, an open-source chat server for mobile and web applications.

faqs.question: Is SAMA free?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAMA is free, open-source software that can be self-hosted; a public hosted demo is also available at app.samacloud.io.

faqs.question: What is SAMA built with?
faqs.answer: The core sama-server is built in JavaScript on uWebSockets.js, with an official React-based web client and a Flutter-based mobile client.

faqs.question: Does SAMA support encryption?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAMA includes end-to-end encryption support along with clustering for high availability.

faqs.question: Who maintains SAMA?
faqs.answer: SAMA is maintained by the SAMA-Communications organization on GitHub, which publishes the server, clients, and an admin dashboard.

faqs.question: Can I test SAMA without installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, the full SAMA stack can be tested against the public cloud instance at app.samacloud.io.

faqs.question: Does SAMA support multiple devices per user?
faqs.answer: Yes, SAMA supports multi-device sessions along with push notifications.

faqs.question: Where can I find SAMA's API documentation?
faqs.answer: SAMA's REST and WebSocket API documentation is published at docs.samacloud.io.

features: React web client and Flutter mobile client

enrichment.short_description: Samba is a free, open-source implementation of the SMB/CIFS networking protocol that lets Unix and Linux systems share files and printers with, and interoperate with, Windows.
enrichment.seo_title: Samba (SMB/CIFS) Review: Features, History, and Use Cases
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Discover how Samba lets Linux and Unix systems share files and printers with Windows, act as an Active Directory domain controller, and more.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Samba is a free, open-source software suite that implements the SMB and CIFS networking protocols, allowing Unix and Linux systems to share files and printers with, and interoperate with, Microsoft Windows clients and servers. The project began when Andrew Tridgell, then a PhD student at the Australian National University, wrote the first version of what would become Samba in December 1991 and January 1992, initially describing it simply as a Unix file server for DOS Pathworks after reverse-engineering the protocol used by DEC Pathworks server software with a packet sniffer. Tridgell soon realized he had implemented the NetBIOS protocol and that the software could interoperate with other PC clients; after early names like netbios for unix and smbserver ran into a trademark conflict with a commercial product named SMBserver, the project was renamed Samba, a name Tridgell found by grepping the Unix system dictionary for words containing the letters S, M, and B in that order. Tridgell marks the adoption of CVS version control in May 1996, alongside growing contributions from developers such as Jeremy Allison, as the point at which the informal project became the Samba Team, the loosely organized global community of volunteers and corporate contributors that has maintained Samba ever since. Major milestones include the 2.0.0 release in January 1999 and the 4.x series, which added full Active Directory domain controller capability alongside traditional file and print sharing. Samba is licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 or later, and LGPLv3 or later for some libraries, having moved from GPLv2 to GPLv3 for all releases starting in the mid-2000s. As a foundational piece of open-source networking infrastructure rather than a commercial product, Samba has no company, pricing, or headquarters; it is developed by volunteers and sponsoring organizations, with recent development partly supported by funding such as the Sovereign Tech Fund. It remains the standard way for Linux, BSD, and macOS systems to join Windows networks, act as file and print servers for Windows clients, or function as a drop-in replacement for a Windows domain controller.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Samba is a free, open-source implementation of the SMB/CIFS protocol, originated by Andrew Tridgell in December 1991 and January 1992 at the Australian National University, and maintained since May 1996 by the volunteer-led Samba Team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Samba lets Unix and Linux systems share files and printers with Windows clients and, since the 4.x series, can act as a full Active Directory domain controller with Kerberos and LDAP services, not just a basic file server.
It also supports classic domain member and NT4-style domain control for legacy environments, and it ships packaged into essentially every major Linux and BSD distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Samba is entirely free and open source, licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 or later, with LGPLv3 or later covering some libraries; there is no company, subscription, or license fee.

features.title: SMB/CIFS File and Print Sharing
features.description: Lets Unix and Linux systems share files and printers with Windows clients and vice versa.

features.title: Active Directory Domain Controller
features.description: Samba 4.x can act as a full Active Directory domain controller, including Kerberos and LDAP services.

features.title: Windows Domain Member Support
features.description: Allows Linux servers to join an existing Windows Active Directory domain as a member server.

features.title: NT4-Style Domain Control
features.description: Legacy support for classic Windows NT-style domain control for older environments.

features.title: Cross-Platform Interoperability
features.description: Works across Linux, BSD, macOS, and Windows, making it a common bridge in mixed-OS networks.

features.title: GPLv3 and LGPLv3 Licensing
features.description: Released under GPLv3 or later, and LGPLv3 or later for some libraries, ensuring the code remains free and open.

features.title: Command-Line and Daemon Tooling
features.description: Includes smbd, nmbd, winbindd, and command-line utilities for configuration, testing, and diagnostics.

features.title: Wide Distribution Support
features.description: Packaged directly into virtually every major Linux and BSD distribution.

pros: Free and open source with no licensing cost, even for enterprise-scale deployments
Mature, battle-tested codebase in continuous development since 1992
Full Active Directory domain controller capability, not just basic file sharing
Packaged into essentially every Linux and Unix distribution, so installation is straightforward
cons: Configuration and Active Directory setup can be complex for administrators unfamiliar with Windows networking internals
No commercial support organization by default; enterprise support requires third-party vendors or distribution-level contracts
SMB protocol version compatibility issues can arise with very old or very new Windows clients
As a community project, release cadence and documentation quality can vary compared with a commercial vendor

faqs.question: What is Samba software used for?
faqs.answer: Samba is used to let Unix and Linux systems share files and printers with Windows computers and to interoperate with, or replace, Windows Active Directory domain controllers.

faqs.question: Is Samba free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Samba is free and open-source software licensed under the GNU General Public License version 3 or later.

faqs.question: Who created Samba?
faqs.answer: Andrew Tridgell wrote the first version of Samba in December 1991 and January 1992 while a PhD student at the Australian National University; the project has since been maintained by the volunteer-led Samba Team.

faqs.question: Where does the name Samba come from?
faqs.answer: Tridgell found the name by running the Unix grep command against the system dictionary looking for words containing the letters S, M, and B in that order, referencing the SMB protocol it implements.

faqs.question: Can Samba act as an Active Directory domain controller?
faqs.answer: Yes, since the Samba 4.x series, Samba can function as a full Active Directory domain controller, not just a file and print server.

faqs.question: What license does Samba use?
faqs.answer: Samba is licensed under GPLv3 or later, having moved from GPLv2, with some libraries under LGPLv3 or later.

faqs.question: Does Samba work on macOS and BSD?
faqs.answer: Yes, Samba runs on Linux, the BSDs, macOS, and other Unix-like systems in addition to being usable from Windows clients.

faqs.question: Is there a company behind Samba?
faqs.answer: No, Samba is maintained by the volunteer, global Samba Team along with contributions and funding from various companies and organizations rather than a single commercial vendor.

use_cases.title: Windows-Linux File Sharing
use_cases.description: System administrators use Samba to let Linux file servers share directories and printers with Windows desktop clients on the same network.

use_cases.title: Active Directory Domain Controller Replacement
use_cases.description: Organizations deploy Samba 4.x as a full Active Directory domain controller, avoiding a Windows Server license for core directory services.
use_cases.audience: IT administrators managing Windows domains on Linux infrastructure

use_cases.title: Cross-Platform Print Serving
use_cases.description: Businesses use Samba to centralize printer sharing so both Linux and Windows machines can print through the same server.
use_cases.audience: Small business and enterprise IT teams

features: Full SMB/CIFS file and print sharing
Domain member and NT4-style domain control
Included in virtually every Linux/Unix distribution

enrichment.short_description: Samvera Hyrax is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails engine that gives libraries and archives a foundation for building digital repository applications.
enrichment.seo_title: Samvera Hyrax Review: Features, Pricing, and Use Cases
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what the open-source Samvera Hyrax digital repository framework offers libraries and archives, from metadata workflows to Valkyrie storage support.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Samvera Hyrax is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails engine that gives libraries, archives, and other cultural heritage and research institutions a ready-made foundation for building digital repository applications to manage, preserve, and provide access to digital collections. Hyrax was created in December 2016 as a consolidation of two earlier, overlapping Samvera community gems, Sufia and CurationConcerns, following community discussion at Hydra Connect 2016 about whether the two projects should merge. Hyrax itself sits within the broader Samvera community, which originated in September 2008 as a collaboration among Stanford University, the University of Virginia, the University of Hull, and the Fedora Commons project under the name the Hydra Project; the community adopted the new name Samvera, Icelandic for togetherness, in May 2017 to reflect its growth beyond the original Hydra-branded tooling. Hyrax is released under the Apache License 2.0 and is maintained collectively by the volunteer Samvera community rather than by a single vendor or company, so there is no pricing, sales process, or corporate headquarters associated with it; institutions typically need their own developers, or a hosting partner, to deploy and customize it. Architecturally, Hyrax provides common repository features out of the box, including deposit workflows, metadata editing, access control, batch operations, collections and works management, and a configurable public-facing discovery interface. More recent major versions, notably Hyrax 5, added support for the Valkyrie data mapper, decoupling the application from a hard dependency on the Fedora repository server so that institutions can choose different persistence and storage back ends. Because it is built specifically for repository and digital-collections use cases rather than general-purpose content management, Hyrax is best suited to academic libraries, archives, museums, and research data repositories that need standards-based metadata, persistent identifiers, and preservation-oriented storage, and it is typically evaluated alongside other Samvera-based tools like Hyku, a hosted, multi-tenant flavor of Hyrax, and other open-source repository platforms such as DSpace and Islandora.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Samvera Hyrax is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails engine, created in December 2016 by consolidating the earlier Sufia and CurationConcerns gems, that gives libraries and archives a foundation for building digital repository applications. It is maintained by the Samvera community, which originated in 2008 as the Hydra Project and was renamed Samvera in May 2017.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hyrax provides deposit and metadata workflows, a works-and-collections data model, role-based access control, batch operations, and a configurable public discovery interface out of the box.
Since Hyrax 5, the framework supports the Valkyrie data mapper, removing the hard dependency on the Fedora repository server so institutions can choose their own persistence and storage back ends.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hyrax is free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, with no vendor pricing; institutions typically need in-house Ruby on Rails developers or a hosting partner to deploy and maintain it.

features.title: Deposit and Metadata Workflows
features.description: Configurable deposit forms and metadata schemas for describing digital objects and collections.

features.title: Works and Collections Model
features.description: Built-in data model for organizing individual works into collections with relationships between items.

features.title: Access Control
features.description: Role- and group-based permissions for controlling who can view, edit, or manage repository content.

features.title: Batch Operations
features.description: Tools for bulk-editing metadata and managing large sets of objects at once.

features.title: Configurable Discovery Interface
features.description: A public-facing search and browse interface that institutions can customize and brand.

features.title: Valkyrie Data Mapper Support
features.description: Since Hyrax 5, a decoupled persistence layer that removes the hard dependency on the Fedora repository server.

features.title: Samvera Ecosystem Compatibility
features.description: Interoperates with other Samvera community tools and gems, including the multi-tenant Hyku distribution.

features.title: Apache 2.0 Licensing
features.description: Fully open-source licensing that allows institutions to freely deploy, modify, and extend the codebase.

pros: Free, open-source, and purpose-built for library and archive digital repository needs
Backed by an active, multi-institution community, Samvera, rather than a single vendor
Flexible persistence options since Hyrax 5 removed the hard Fedora dependency via Valkyrie
Proven at scale across many academic and cultural heritage institutions worldwide
cons: Requires in-house Ruby on Rails development expertise or a hosting partner to deploy and maintain
No official pricing or vendor support line, since it is entirely community-maintained
Setup and metadata modeling can be complex for institutions without prior repository experience
Feature roadmap and release cadence depend on volunteer community capacity rather than a dedicated product team

faqs.question: What is Samvera Hyrax?
faqs.answer: Samvera Hyrax is a free, open-source Ruby on Rails engine that provides libraries, archives, and research institutions with a foundation for building digital repository applications.

faqs.question: When was Hyrax created?
faqs.answer: Hyrax was created in December 2016 as a consolidation of two earlier Samvera community gems, Sufia and CurationConcerns.

faqs.question: What license does Hyrax use?
faqs.answer: Hyrax is released under the Apache License 2.0.

faqs.question: What is the Samvera community?
faqs.answer: Samvera is a global community of libraries, archives, and technologists that maintains Hyrax and related repository tools; it originated in 2008 as the Hydra Project and was renamed Samvera, Icelandic for togetherness, in May 2017.

faqs.question: Does Hyrax require Fedora?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily; since Hyrax 5, the framework supports the Valkyrie data mapper, which decouples the application from a hard dependency on the Fedora repository server.

faqs.question: Is there a hosted version of Hyrax?
faqs.answer: Institutions can self-host Hyrax or use Hyku, a multi-tenant Samvera distribution built on Hyrax, or work with third-party hosting partners.

faqs.question: Who uses Samvera Hyrax?
faqs.answer: Academic libraries, archives, museums, and research data repositories use Hyrax to manage and provide access to digital collections.

faqs.question: Is Hyrax free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hyrax is free, open-source software with no licensing cost, though institutions typically need developer resources to deploy and customize it.

use_cases.title: Institutional Digital Repositories
use_cases.description: Academic libraries use Hyrax to build institutional repositories for theses, research data, and digitized special collections.

use_cases.title: Archival Collections Access
use_cases.description: Archives and museums use Hyrax's works-and-collections model to describe, organize, and provide public access to digitized archival materials.

use_cases.title: Multi-Tenant Repository Hosting
use_cases.description: Consortia and hosting providers build on Hyrax via the Hyku distribution to offer repository-as-a-service to multiple smaller institutions.
use_cases.audience: Library consortia and repository hosting providers

features: Full Hyrax Ruby on Rails engine source code
Works, collections, and access control model
Valkyrie data mapper support in Hyrax 5 and later
Apache 2.0 license, free to modify and deploy

enrichment.short_description: SANE, short for Scanner Access Now Easy, is a free, open-source API and driver framework that provides standardized access to scanners on Unix and Linux systems.
enrichment.seo_title: SANE (Scanner Access Now Easy) Review: Features and Use Cases
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Understand what the open-source SANE API offers for scanning on Linux and Unix, including backends, scanimage, and network scanning with saned.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SANE, an acronym for Scanner Access Now Easy, is a free and open-source application programming interface and driver framework that provides a standardized way for software on Unix and Unix-like operating systems, including Linux, BSD, and macOS, to access image scanners, still and video cameras, and frame grabbers. The project's first public release came on November 27, 1996, originated by David Mosberger-Tang together with early collaborators, after preliminary development work that had begun a few years earlier; the project has since been maintained by a volunteer community rather than a company. SANE's architecture separates hardware-specific backends, which speak directly to individual scanner models or families over USB, SCSI, or network connections, from generic frontends, the applications end users interact with; the reference command-line frontend, scanimage, and the network scanning daemon, saned, ship as part of the core sane-backends package, while graphical frontends such as XSane and xscanimage are developed and distributed separately. The saned network daemon allows a scanner physically attached to one machine to be shared and accessed remotely over a network, extending SANE's usefulness beyond a single desktop. SANE components are distributed under GPL and LGPL licenses, with some libraries having moved from the Library GPL to LGPL 2.1 over the years, consistent with its status as core open-source Unix infrastructure rather than a commercial product; it has no list price, subscription plans, company, or headquarters. Source code and issue tracking for the backends now live in a GitLab repository under the sane-project namespace, and SANE is packaged directly into essentially every major Linux distribution, making it the de facto standard scanning layer beneath higher-level tools like GIMP's scanner plugin, common print-and-scan utilities, and desktop scanning applications. SANE is best suited to Linux and Unix system administrators, distribution packagers, and developers who need a hardware-abstracted way to add scanning support to an application, rather than end users looking for a polished consumer scanning app, though tools like XSane provide a usable graphical front end for everyday scanning tasks.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: SANE, short for Scanner Access Now Easy, is a free, open-source API and driver framework first released on November 27, 1996 by David Mosberger-Tang and early collaborators, providing standardized scanner access across Unix and Linux systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: SANE separates hardware-specific backend drivers from generic frontend applications, with the command-line scanimage tool and the saned network scanning daemon included in the core sane-backends package.

Separately distributed graphical frontends such as XSane and xscanimage sit on top of the same API, and SANE underpins scanning support in tools like GIMP's scanner plugin across virtually every major Linux distribution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SANE is free and open source, distributed under GPL and LGPL licenses, with no company, list price, or subscription plans; its source code is hosted publicly on GitLab under the sane-project namespace.

features.title: Standardized Scanner API
features.description: A common programming interface that applications use to talk to scanners without needing device-specific code.

features.title: Hardware Backends
features.description: Dedicated backend drivers for hundreds of scanner models across USB, SCSI, and network connections.

features.title: scanimage Command-Line Frontend
features.description: A flexible command-line tool for scanning to PNM, TIFF, and other formats, included in sane-backends.

features.title: saned Network Daemon
features.description: A network scanning daemon that lets a scanner attached to one machine be shared and accessed remotely.

features.title: Graphical Frontends
features.description: Separately distributed graphical applications such as XSane and xscanimage built on the SANE API.

features.title: GPL and LGPL Licensing
features.description: Backends and tools distributed under GPL, with some libraries under LGPL, keeping the stack fully open.

features.title: Broad Linux Distribution Support
features.description: Packaged into essentially every major Linux distribution as the default scanning layer.

features.title: Application Integration
features.description: Underpins scanning support in tools like GIMP's scanner plugin and common desktop print-and-scan utilities.

pros: Free, open-source, and installed by default across nearly all major Linux distributions
Broad hardware support through hundreds of community-maintained backend drivers
Network scanning support via saned allows shared scanners across a network
Long track record of stability as core Unix/Linux scanning infrastructure since 1996
cons: No official company, support contract, or guaranteed response time for driver issues
Backend support for brand-new scanner models can lag behind vendor Windows and macOS driver releases
Command-line and API-level tools require more technical comfort than typical consumer scanning software
Documentation is spread across the project website, man pages, and distribution wikis rather than centralized

faqs.question: What does SANE stand for?
faqs.answer: SANE stands for Scanner Access Now Easy, an open-source API for accessing scanners and imaging hardware on Unix and Linux systems.

faqs.question: Is SANE free?
faqs.answer: Yes, SANE is free and open-source software distributed under GPL and LGPL licenses.

faqs.question: When was SANE first released?
faqs.answer: SANE's first public release was on November 27, 1996, originated by David Mosberger-Tang and early collaborators.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SANE backends and frontends?
faqs.answer: Backends are hardware-specific drivers that communicate with individual scanner models, while frontends, such as scanimage or XSane, are the applications end users interact with to actually perform a scan.

faqs.question: Can SANE scan over a network?
faqs.answer: Yes, the saned daemon allows a scanner attached to one computer to be accessed and controlled remotely over a network.

faqs.question: Does SANE work with all scanners?
faqs.answer: SANE supports scanners through individually maintained backend drivers, so hardware compatibility depends on whether a backend exists for a given model; support for very new consumer scanners can lag.

faqs.question: Where is SANE's source code hosted?
faqs.answer: The sane-backends source code and issue tracker are hosted on GitLab under the sane-project namespace.

faqs.question: What applications use SANE?
faqs.answer: Applications like GIMP's scanner plugin, XSane, and many Linux print-and-scan utilities use SANE as their underlying scanning layer.

use_cases.title: Linux Desktop Scanning
use_cases.description: Desktop Linux users scan documents and photos using graphical frontends like XSane or GIMP's scanner plugin, both built on the SANE API.

use_cases.title: Scripted and Automated Scanning
use_cases.description: System administrators and developers use the scanimage command-line frontend to script batch scanning jobs on Unix and Linux servers.

use_cases.title: Shared Network Scanning
use_cases.description: Offices with a scanner attached to one Linux machine use the saned daemon to let multiple users scan remotely over the network.
use_cases.audience: Small office and lab environments running Linux

features: Full SANE API and backend driver source code
GPL/LGPL licensed, free to modify and redistribute
